_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Universal Care: Patient Refusals. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Universal Care: Patient Refusals
aliases: Against medical advice Refusal of treatment Refusal of transport

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: An adult or emancipated minor who has demonstrated possessing sufficient mental capacity for making decisions has the right to determine the course of his/her medical care, including the refusal of care
Individuals must be advised of the risks and consequences resulting from refusal of medical care to enable an informed decision regarding consent or refusal of treatment

An individual determined to lack decision-making capacity by EMS clinicians should not be allowed to refuse care against medical advice or to be released at the scene. Mental illness, drugs, alcohol intoxication, or physical/mental impairment may significantly impair an individual's decision-making capacity. Individuals who have attempted suicide, verbalized suicidal intent, or had other factors that lead EMS clinicians to suspect suicidal intent, should not be regarded as having demonstrated sufficient decision-making capacity
The determination of decision-making capacity may be challenged by communication barriers or cultural differences
EMS clinicians should not put themselves in danger by attempting to treat and/or transport an individual who refuses care. Law enforcement personnel should be requested if needed
Always act in the best interest of the patient. EMS clinicians, with the support of direct medical oversight, must strike a balance between abandoning the patient and forcing care

Special Considerations ' Minors It is preferable for minors to have a parent or legal guardian who can provide consent for treatment on behalf of the child a. All states allow healthcare clinicians to provide emergency treatment when a parent is not available to provide consent. This is known as the emergency exception rule or the doctrine of implied consent. For minors, this doctrine means that the EMS clinician can presume consent and proceed with appropriate treatment and transport if the following six conditions are met: i. The child is suffering from an emergent condition that places their life or health in danger

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Document patient capacity with: o All barriers to patient care in the NEMSIS element 'eHistory.01'Barriers to Patient Care' (a Required National Element of NEMSIS)
o Exam fields for 'eExam.19'Mental Status' and 'eExam.20'Neurological Assessment'
o Vitals for level of responsiveness and Glasgow Coma Scale
o Alcohol and drug use indicators
o Blood glucose level (as appropriate to situation and patient history)
' Patient Age
' Minors who are not emancipated and adults with a legal guardian: guardian name, contact, and relationship
' Any efforts made to contact guardians if contact could not be made

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
